Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty loose. I'd try to carve out maybe an hour or two here and there throughout the week, but with work and everything else going on, it wasn't consistent. I'm a visual learner, so I'd focus on watching the videos and looking at diagrams when I could. Most of my studying happened late at night when things quieted down, but I wouldn't call it organized really.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was due. I'd go days without touching course material, then cram when deadlines approached. Being 27 and having responsibilities outside of school means I can't always stick to a perfect schedule. Some weeks I'd manage regular sessions, other weeks life just got in the way and I'd have to catch up all at once.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or seeing a notification that something new was posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I was missing anything important. To be honest, I wasn't always motivated to go in there unless I had a specific reason. The peer support group would sometimes nudge me too, which helped when I was feeling disconnected from the course.
Q4: There were a few weeks where I got into a rhythm of checking in every couple of days, especially when I was keeping up with the video lectures. It became routine when I started associating it with my evening wind-down time. But that habit would break whenever stress from other areas of life piled up. The routine never really stuck permanently for me.
Q5: The video content was definitely the most useful for me since I learn best visually. Being able to pause, rewind, and watch things at my own pace made a big difference. I also found the peer interactions helpful when I could bounce ideas off classmates. The visual aids and diagrams in the lectures helped concepts click in ways that reading alone never did for me.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums entirely. I just didn't have the energy to post there, and honestly it felt like busy work to me. Some of the reading materials I'd skim or skip altogether if I felt like the videos covered the same content. When you're low on time and resources, you have to prioritize what actually helps you learn.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to the assignments or exams. If it helped me understand something I was confused about, that made it valuable. I also appreciated things that didn't take forever—quick, focused content that got to the point. Anything that felt like filler or repetition just made me tune out.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt like a luxury I couldn't afford most of the time. Required activities got done because they had to, but optional ones only happened if I had extra energy, which was rare. I'd tell myself I'd get to the optional stuff later, but later never really came. There's a mental difference when something is required versus suggested.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download PDFs and videos to study offline because my internet isn't always reliable and I can't always afford to stream everything. I'd review my notes on paper, make my own diagrams, and study from those. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer, just me with printed materials or notes I'd written out.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was going over my handwritten notes or reviewing downloaded content where the LMS wouldn't see any of that. I'd spend an hour studying from printouts at work during my break, but that's invisible to the system. Same with when I'd discuss concepts with peers in person or over text—real learning happening, zero digital footprint.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once online, maybe download it, and then revisit it multiple times offline. Same with lecture slides—I'd pull them up once, save them, and refer back to them repeatedly without logging in again. It was just more practical for my situation with limited resources and unreliable internet access.
Q12: Mid-semester was rough. Work got more demanding, and I was dealing with some personal stress that just drained me. My motivation, which was already not great, hit rock bottom. I'd look at the course material and just feel bored and overwhelmed at the same time. The peer support helped a bit, but there were weeks where I barely engaged at all.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the last thing I wanted to do was log into the LMS. Fatigue made everything feel harder than it needed to be. Some weeks I'd only do the bare minimum to not fall completely behind. Competing responsibilities always won when it came down to choosing between coursework and real-life obligations.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd be scrolling through materials but my brain was somewhere else entirely. I'd have a video playing but I wasn't really absorbing anything. Those sessions felt pointless in retrospect. I was physically present on the platform but mentally checked out, just going through the motions to feel like I was doing something.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even when I knew they mattered. Part of it was low motivation—I just couldn't make myself care enough in the moment. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed and not knowing where to start. Avoidance became easier than facing the work, which obviously made things worse when deadlines hit.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main motivator. I'd see a due date approaching and suddenly find the energy to engage. Without deadlines, I'd probably drift along doing nothing. They created urgency that my low motivation couldn't generate on its own. Most of my LMS activity probably clustered right before things were due.
Q17: Feedback had some effect, but honestly I didn't always check it right away. When I did see positive feedback, it gave me a small boost. Critical feedback sometimes made me want to disengage more because it felt discouraging when I was already struggling. The peer support helped me process feedback better than I would have on my own.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but it felt like a lot of content spread across too many modules. Having 20 online courses worth of material meant I was always playing catch-up. The structure didn't really accommodate people like me who have limited time and resources. I needed things to be more streamlined and focused.
Q19: The video content being available on-demand made things easier since I could work around my schedule. But the sheer volume of material made engagement harder. Also, the discussion requirements felt like they didn't fit my learning style at all. I would have engaged more if there were more visual, interactive elements rather than text-heavy forums.
Q20: I think sometimes people are exploring, trying to figure out what they need to focus on, or they're confused about what's actually required. High activity might also mean someone is struggling and repeatedly reviewing things without making progress. Or maybe they're dealing with stuff outside of school that prevents them from completing work even though they're trying.
Q21: That sounds like me in some ways. I studied a lot offline, so my LMS activity doesn't reflect my actual effort. You can learn effectively without constantly being logged in if you're using downloaded materials or other resources. Performance isn't always tied to how much you click around on a platform—it's about what you actually absorb and apply.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and what you access, which says something about habits. But they miss the quality of engagement and all the offline learning that happens. They can't measure understanding, effort, or what's going on in someone's life that affects their participation. It's a partial picture at best.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine effort to understand it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials—it's being mentally present and curious. For me, engagement also means finding ways to connect the content to something meaningful, even when motivation is low. It's an internal state that external metrics can't fully capture.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied from printed notes or downloaded files. It wouldn't capture the conversations I had with peers outside the platform or the mental effort I put in during my commute thinking about concepts. It also can't show how stress and limited resources shaped my engagement, or how the peer support intervention actually helped me stay afloat emotionally.
